Influenza
CLS-N390 Vaccines: S c ience and Society

Influenza Virus
Single stranded, helical shaped, RNA virus
Lipid envelope
Capsid
Found worldwide, year round
Influenza A = most common
Influenza B = milder, second wave
Influenza C = rare in humans
Influenza D = does not infect humans
Several major pandemic

Influenza A Virus Structure
Two important proteins
Hemagglutinin (H) – 18 types
Neuraminidase (N) – 11 types
H1N1
198 different influenza A combinations
Only 131 have been found

Transmission
Person to person
Coughing
Sneezing
Talking
Droplets land on mouth or nose – 6 feet away
May be inhaled when droplets aerosolize
Much less likely – surface contamination
Most contagious – first 3-4 days of illness
Infectious one day before symptoms, up to 5-7days after becoming sick
R 0 value = 1 – 2

Symptoms
Begin 1 – 4 days after virus enters
Typically come on suddenly
Fever or feeling feverish/chills
Cough
Sore throat
Runny or stuffy nose
Muscle or body aches
Headache
Fatigue
Vomiting and diarrhea – more common in children

Complications
Pneumonia – common complication
Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, Staphylococcus aureus
Reye Syndrome – seen in children taking aspirin
Severe vomiting and confusion  may progress to coma due to swelling of brain
Myocarditis – inflammation of heart
Death – reported in less than 1 in 1,000 cases
Typically seen in >65
Vaccine

Influenza Vaccines in US
Inactivated influenza vaccine (IIV)
Live attenuated influenza vaccine (LAIV)
Flu strains seen in Australia during our spring to summer determine which strains will be in the United States flu vaccine for winter

Inactivated Influenza Vaccine (IIV)
Been around since 1940s
Multiple manufacturers make IIV
Quadrivalent -  A(H1N1), A9H3N2), two B
Grown in chicken eggs – severe allergic reactions with egg allergies
Dose varies by age and manufacturer

Inactivated Influenza Vaccine (IIV)
Afluria Quadrivalent
Seqirus
0.25 mL syringe – 6mo – 35mo
0.5 mL syringe – 3+
5.0 mL vial – 6mo+ (needle); 18-64 (jet injector)
Fluarix Quadrivalent
GlaxoSmithKline
0.5 mL syringe – 6mo+
FluLaval Quadrivalent
GlaxoSmithKline
0.5 mL syringe – 6mo+
Quadrivalent, standard dose, intramuscular injection
Egg-based
Fluzone Quadrivalent
Sanofi Pasteur
0.5 mL syringe – 6mo+
0.5 mL vial – 6mo+
5.0 mL vial – 6mo+

Inactivated Influenza Vaccine (IIV)
Fluzone High Dose Quadrivalent
Sanofi Pasture
0.7 mL syringe – 65+
Quadrivalent, standard dose, intramuscular injection
Egg-based
High dose – 4x virus component of standard dose

Inactivated Influenza Vaccine (IIV)
Fluad Quadrivalent
Seqirus
0.5 mL syringe – 65+
Quadrivalent, standard dose, intramuscular injection
Egg-based
MF59 adjuvant – oil in water emulsion for increased absorbance

Inactivated Influenza Vaccine (IIV)
Flucelvax Quadrivalent
Seqirus
0.5 mL syringe – 6mo+
5.0 mL vial – 6mo+
Quadrivalent, standard dose, intramuscular injection
Cell culture-based ( ccIIV ) – no egg issues
Made in Madin Darby Canine Kidney (MDCK) cells

Inactivated Influenza Vaccine (IIV)
Flublok Quadrivalent
Sanofi Pasteur
0.5 mL syringe – 18+
Quadrivalent, standard dose, intramuscular injection
Recombinant (RIV) hemagglutinin used  - no egg concerns

Live Attenuated Influenza Vaccine (LAIV)
FluMist Quadrivalent
AstraZeneca
0.2 mL intranasal sprayer – 2-49
0.1 mL in each nostril
Quadrivalent, intranasal
Egg-based
Influenza Activity
Influenza Activity
Pandemics

1889-1890: Flu Pandemic
Originally called the “Asiatic Flu” or “Russian Flu”
Influenza A virus subtype H3N8 .
The first cases were observed in May 1889 in three separate locations
Bukhara in Central Asia
Athabasca in NW Canada
Greenland.
Rapid population growth of the 19th century, specifically in urban areas, helped the flu spread, and before long the outbreak had spread across the globe.
Though it was the first true epidemic in the era of bacteriology and much was learned from it.
Death Toll: 1 million
US – 10,481 deaths

1918-1920: Flu Pandemic
Wire service reports of a flu outbreak in Madrid in the spring of 1918 led to the pandemic being called the “Spanish flu.”
The pandemic was caused by Influenza A subtype H1N1
Over 1/3 of the world’s population was infected.
The 1918 flu pandemic was different from other influenza outbreaks because it was notorious for striking down hardy and completely healthy young adults, while leaving children and those with weaker immune systems still alive.
Death toll: 20 – 50 million
Mortality rate estimated at 10% - 20%
Up to 25 million deaths in first 25 weeks alone

1956-1958: Asian Flu
Asian Flu was a pandemic outbreak of Influenza A subtype H2N2 , that originated in China in 1956 and lasted until 1958.
In its two-year spree, Asian Flu traveled from the Chinese province of Guizhou to Singapore, Hong Kong, and the United States.
Death toll: 2 million
69,800 of those in the US alone.

1968: Flu Pandemic
A category 2 Flu pandemic sometimes referred to as “the Hong Kong Flu,” the 1968 flu pandemic was caused by Influenza A subtype H3N2, a genetic offshoot of the H2N2 subtype.
From the first reported case on July 13, 1968 in Hong Kong, it took only 17 days before outbreaks of the virus were reported in Singapore and Vietnam, and within three months had spread to The Philippines, India, Australia, Europe, and the United States.
Death toll: 1 million
Low mortality rate = 0.5%
500,000 residents of Hong Kong, approximately 15% of its population at the time.

2009-2010: Influenza Pandemic
The virus appeared to be a new strain of Influenza A subtype H1N1 that resulted from a previous triple reassortment of bird, swine, and human flu viruses and that further combined with a Eurasian pig flu virus.
Nearly 1/3 of adults over 60 had antibodies because of historic exposure to 1918 Spanish flu.
Most young people didn’t
Vaccine produced, but not available until 11/2009
Death toll: 151,700-575,400
US (4/12/09-4/10/10) – 60.8 million cases, 274,304 hospitalizations, 12,469 deaths

COVID-19
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and Society

SARS-CoV-2
D iscovered in 2019
C oronavirus = proteins look like crown
Four coronaviruses cause normal cold-li ke respiratory illness
Same family as
SARS
MERS
F ound worldwide
Data from June 3, 2022

Symptoms
Wide range of symptoms – 2-14 days after exposure
Cough
Fever or chills
Nausea or vomiting
Congestion or runny nose
Sore throat
New loss of taste or smell
Headache
Muscle or body aches
Diarrhea
Fatigue
Shortness of breath or difficulty breathing

Transmission
Spread from infected person’s mouth or nose in small liquid particles
Coughing
Sneezing
Speaking
Singing
Breathing
Surface survival limited to approximately 72 hours depending on surface
Vaccine

COVID-19 V accine Timeline
Sputnik V
Russian fast-tracked vaccine
8/20
12/20
7/20
2/21
mRNA vaccines
FDA appoves Moderna and Pfizer-BioNTech
Coronavac
Clinical trials begin at Moderna and Pfizer-BioNTech
China lauched clinical triaals of inactivated virus vaccine
Viral vector
FDA approves Johnson & Jonson Janssen vaccine

COMIRNATY - Pfizer-BioNTech
mRNA encoding viral spike glycoprotein of SARS-CoV-2
Approved for anyone 5 years and older
2 dose primary series, given 3-8 weeks apart
Booster recommended for most; some second booster
Does not contain
Eggs
Preservatives
Latex
Metals
Ingredients
Lipids (fats) help mRNA enter cells
Sugars and salts – stabilizers during manufacturing, freezing, shipping, and storing
Rare risk of myocarditis and pericarditis, mostly in males 12-39

Spikevax – Moderna
mRNA encoding viral spike glycoprotein of SARS-CoV-2
Approved for anyone 18 years and older
2 dose primary series, given 4-8 weeks apart
Booster recommended for most; some second booster
Does not contain
Eggs
Preservatives
Latex
Metals
Ingredients
Lipids (fats) help mRNA enter cells
Sugars, salts, acetic acid – stabilizers during manufacturing, freezing, shipping, and storing
Rare risk of myocarditis and pericarditis, mostly in males 12-39

Johnson & Johnson / Janssen COVID-19 Vaccine
Inactivated adenovirus containing stabilized variant of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein
Approved for anyone 18 years and older
1 dose given
Booster of mRNA vaccine recommended
Does not contain
Eggs
Preservatives
Latex
Metals
Ingredients
Sugars, salts, citric acid, ethanol – stabilizers during manufacturing, shipping, and storing
Risk of blood clots with low platelet count (thrombosis with thrombocytopenia syndrome – TTS) - 3.83 per million doses

https://covid.cdc.gov/covid-data-tracker/#datatracker-home [https://covid.cdc.gov/covid-data-tracker/#datatracker-home]
Keep up with COVID-19 Data

Haemophilus influenzae type B
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and Society

Tiny Gram negative bacilli
Picture = cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
Dark circles at top = white blood cells
Small specks = bacteria
Needs 5% - 10% CO 2 and media with extra nutrients – most labs can grow!
Part of normal bacteria (flora) in upper respiratory tract
Hangs out in nasal passages and back of throat
Haemophilus influenzae

Haemophilus influenzae
Different subtypes of H. influenzae known as “a” through “f”
Determined by sugar molecules on surface of bacteria
Most subtypes do not cause bad infections
Type B can become invasive
gets into sterile areas such as blood and CSF

Infect yourself
Normal bacteria in sinus or throat = no problem
Passes through sinuses to inner ear = infection
Makes it into lungs = infection
Infect others
Spread via respiratory droplets – coughing and sneezing
May be spread by carriers who are not actually sick – most common
R 0 value of H. influenzae type B =  estimate at 3.3
Transmission

Common cause of
Inner ear infections in children
Bronchitis in adults
Symptoms
Invasive infections
Pneumonia
Bloodstream infections
Meningitis
Epiglottitis
Cellulitis
Infectious arthritis

Most often in babies and children <5
Adults 65+, American Indians, Alaskan Natives, and people with:
Sickle cell disease
Asplenia (no spleen)
HIV infection
Antibody and complement deficiency syndromes (rare conditions that affect the body’s ability to fight infections)
Cancer requiring chemotherapy, radiation, or bone marrow stem cell transplant
At Risk Populations
Vaccine

Haemophilus influenzae type B (Hib) Vaccine
Polysaccharide-protein conjugate vaccine
Type B sugar from bacteria bound to protein that carries it
T lymphocytes usually ignore sugars – protein make it more appealing

Haemophilus influenzae type B (Hib) Vaccine
Type | Vaccine | 2 months | 4 months | 6 months | 12-15 months
PRP-T | ActHIB | X | X | X | X
Pentacel | X | X | X | X
Hiberix | - | - | - | X
MenHibrix | X | X | X | X
PRP-OMP | PedvaxHIB | X | X | - | X
COMVAX | X | X | - | X
PRP-T = bound to tetanus protein or toxoid
PRP-OMP = bound to Neisseria meningitidis outer membrane protein

Haemophilus influenzae type B Combination Vaccines
HepB -Hib-PRP-OMP (Merck)
Combines Hepatitis B and Hib
Cannot give to children <6 weeks
DTaP-IPV-Hib-PRP-T (Sanofi Pasture)
Contains lyophilized Hib vaccine that is reconstituted with liquid DTaP-IPV vaccine
FDA approved for doses 1-4 of DTaP series
Hib- MenCY ( GlascoSmithKlein )
Lyophilized, reconstitute with 0.9% saline
Haemophilus influenzae Invasive Disease
Haemophilus influenzae Invasive Disease

Haemophilus influenzae Invasive Disease
2015 - 2019

Meningococcal Disease
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and Society

Gram negative diplococci (kidney bean shape)
Picture shows bacteria eaten by neutrophil
Same genus as N. gonorrhoeae
Six serogroups – A, B, C, W, X, and Y
Three cause most illness in US – B, C, Y
Neisseria meningitidis

Transmission
Approximately 10% of population naturally has in back of throat = carrier
Spread via respiratory and throat secretions
Requires close or lengthy contact
Not as contagious as cold or flu
Cannot get from air
At higher risk:
Children <1, adolescents, 16 – 23
Group settings – colleges
Immunocompromised individuals

Symptoms
Incubation time: 2 – 10 days (3 – 4 more often)
Meningococcal Meningitis | Meningococcal Septicemia
Most common symptoms:
Fever 
Headache 
Stiff neck 

Often additional symptoms:
Nausea
Vomiting
Photophobia (eye sensitivity to light)
Altered mental status (confusion) | Bacteria enter bloodstream, damaging walls of blood vessels. Causes bleeding into the skin and organs

Symptoms:
Fever and chills 
Fatigue
Vomiting 
Cold hands and feet 
Severe aches or pains in muscles, joints, chest, or abdomen 
Rapid beathing 
Diarrhea 
Dark purple rash
Vaccine

Meningococcal Vaccine
First licensed – 1974
Current version licensed – 1981
Polysaccharide-protein conjugate vaccine
A, C, W, Y
Typically give 11-12 with booster at 16
Or one dose at 13-15 if not given earlier

Meningococcal Vaccine
Menactra
(Sanofi Pasture)
MenACWY -D
Licensed in 2005
Single dose vial
No preservatives or adjuvants
Protein carrier = diphtheria toxoid
Approved for 9 months – 55 years
Menveo (Novartis)
MenACWY -CRM
Licensed in 2010
MenA component – lyophilized
MenC,W,Y in saline – used to dilute MenA portion
No preservatives or adjuvants
Protein carrier = cross reacting material of diphtheria toxoid
Approved for 2-55
MenHibrix (GlaxoSmithKline)
Hib- MenCY -TT
Licensed in 2012
MenCY
H. influenzae type B
Lyophilized – dilute with 0.9% saline
Protein carrier = tetanus toxoid
Approved as 4 dose series – 2, 4, 6, and 12-18 months

Pneumococcal Disease
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and Society

Gram positive cocci in chains -- Often hang out in pairs -- Lancet shape (peanut) Same genus as strep throat ( S. pyogenes ) and neonatal meningitis ( S. agalactiae ) 92 serotypes --10 most common cause about 62% of invasive disease worldwide
Streptococcus pneumoniae

Transmission
Found all over -- Normal bacteria in back of throat for some – carrier
Spread via direct contact with respiratory secretions – saliva and mucous
Many people have had it in nose or throat without being ill
Age and heath conditions increase risk for pneumococcal disease

At Risk
Children at risk | Adults as risk
Younger than 2 years old and those with: 
Sickle cell disease, no spleen, HIV infection, cancer, or another condition that weakens the immune system
Diabetes
Nephrotic syndrome
Chronic heart, lung, or kidney disease
Cochlear implants
Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leaks (escape of the fluid that surrounds the brain and spinal cord) | Adults 65+

Adults with: 
Sickle cell disease, no spleen, HIV infection, cancer, or another condition that weakens the immune system
Diabetes
Nephrotic syndrome
Chronic heart, lung, kidney, or liver disease
Cochlear implants
CSF leaks
Alcoholism

Adults who smoke cigarettes or have chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), emphysema, and asthma

Symptoms
May cause many different types of infections
Middle ear infection
Sinus infection
Pneumoniae
Meningitis
Bloodstream infection
Sepsis

Sinus infection
Middle ear infection
Symptoms
Common symptoms include:
Headache
Stuffy or runny nose
Loss of sense of smell
Facial pain or pressure
Postnasal drip
Complications are rare, by may be:
Infection of tissue surrounding eye
Bone infection
Abscess (collection of pus)
Common symptoms of otitis media include:
Ear pain
A red, swollen ear drum
Fever
Sleepiness
Usually mild and more common than severe forms of disease
Children with repeated ear infections may need ear tubes

Pneumonia
Symptoms
Common symptoms include:
Fever and chills
Cough
Rapid breathing or difficulty breathing
Chest pain
Older adults may experience confusion or low alertness rather than common symptoms
Complications of pneumococcal pneumonia include:
Infection of space between membranes that surround lungs and chest cavity (empyema)
Inflammation of sac surrounding heart (pericarditis)
Blockage of airway allowing air into the lungs with collapse within the lungs and collection of pus in the lungs
Pneumococcal pneumonia kills about 1 in 20 who get it

Blood infection
Sepsis
Symptoms
Symptoms of pneumococcal bacteremia include:
Fever
Chills
Low alertness
About 1 in 30 children and 1 in 7 adults die of pneumococcal bacteremia
Survivors may have loss of limb(s)
Sepsis is an extreme response to a blood infection. Symptoms include:
Confusion or disorientation
Shortness of breath
High heart rate
Fever, shivering, or feeling very cold
Extreme pain or discomfort
Clammy or sweaty skin
Complications include kidney failure and damage to brain, lungs, or heart

Meningitis
Symptoms
Symptoms of pneumococcal meningitis include:
Stiff neck
Fever
Headache
Photophobia
Confusion
In babies, meningitis may cause poor eating and drinking, low alertness and vomiting
About 1 in 12 children and 1 in 7 adults die of pneumococcal meningitis
Survivors may have long-term problems – hearing loss or developmental delays
Vaccine

Pneumococcal Vaccine Timeline
1977
1983
2000
2010
14-valent polysaccharide vaccine licensed
23-valent polysaccharide vaccine licensed
7-valent polysaccharide conjugate vaccine licensed
13-valent polysaccharide conjugate vaccine licensed
2011
13-valent vaccine approved for 50+

Pneumococcal Vaccines
Pneumovax 23 (Merck)
23-valent polysaccharide
23 types of bacteria causing 60%-76% of invasive disease
Single vial or 5 dose vial
Phenol preservative
For 2 yrs +
60% - 70% effective
Less effective against pneumococcal pneumonia
7 valent polysaccharide conjugate
7 types of bacteria
Conjugated to nontoxic variant of diphtheria toxin (CRM197)
13-valent polysaccharide conjugate
13 types of bacteria
Conjugated to CRM197
Polysorbate 80 – surfactant
Aluminum phosphate – adjuvant
Succinate buffer
No thimerosal preservative
